title: Geometry OS Roadmap
description:
GPU-native operating system where pixels are instructions, the GPU texture is memory,
and programs write programs. Implemented in Rust (~19,400 lines) with 347 tests passing
on real hardware (NVIDIA RTX 5090, 32GB VRAM).

phases.title: Bootstrap
phases.description:
The foundation. Rust binary that initializes wgpu, creates the 4096x4096 RGBA8
texture (64MB), and proves the GPU pipeline works end-to-end.
phases.goal: Rust project skeleton with wgpu initialization and a 4096x4096 texture.

deliverables.name: wgpu initialization
deliverables.description: Create GPU device, queue, and 4096x4096 RGBA8 texture

acceptance_criteria.description: Texture created and accessible via compute shader

deliverables.name: Cargo project structure
deliverables.description: Multi-binary Cargo.toml with daemon, repl, debug targets

acceptance_criteria.description: cargo build succeeds for all targets

phases.title: Pixel VM Core
phases.description:
The VM reads RGBA pixels as (opcode, stratum, p1, p2) and executes them.
8 concurrent VMs, each with 128 registers, 64-level call stack, program counter.
Software VM (software_vm.rs) mirrors the shader exactly for cross-validation.
phases.goal: Fetch-decode-execute loop that reads opcodes from pixel values in the texture.

deliverables.name: Compute shader VM (glyph_vm_scheduler.wgsl)
deliverables.description: GPU compute shader that fetches, decodes, and executes pixel opcodes

acceptance_criteria.description: All basic opcodes execute correctly on GPU

deliverables.name: Software VM mirror (software_vm.rs)
deliverables.description: CPU-side VM that produces identical results to the shader

acceptance_criteria.description: Cross-validated: same inputs produce same outputs on CPU and GPU

deliverables.name: Opcode set (35+ opcodes)
deliverables.description: NOP, LDI, MOV, LOAD, STORE, ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, JMP, BNE, CALL, RET, HALT, ENTRY, CHAR, BLIT, SEND, RECV, SPAWN, YIELD, WAIT_EVENT, XOR, NOT, MOD, LDB, STB, RECTF, LINE, TEXT_STR, CIRCLEF, and more

acceptance_criteria.description: Each opcode has dedicated tests

deliverables.name: 8 concurrent VMs
deliverables.description: Parallel execution with per-VM register file, PC, call stack

acceptance_criteria.description: All 8 VMs can execute simultaneously

phases.technical_notes:
The pixel encoding: R=opcode (0-255), G=stratum (layer/mode/condition),
B=parameter 1, A=parameter 2. Each VM gets a spatial region of the texture.

phases.title: Spatial Memory
phases.description:
Memory management via 2D spatial coordinates mapped to 1D via Hilbert curve.
Each VM gets an isolated region bounded by base_addr/bound_addr.
Out-of-bounds access triggers VM_FAULT.
phases.goal: Hilbert curve mapping and spatial memory allocation with bounds checking.

deliverables.name: Hilbert curve mapping
deliverables.description: 2D texture coords <-> 1D physical addresses via Hilbert curve

acceptance_criteria.description: Spatial locality preserved in physical cache layout

deliverables.name: Spatial memory allocation
deliverables.description: VM isolation via base_addr/bound_addr bounds checking

acceptance_criteria.description: Out-of-bounds access triggers VM_FAULT

deliverables.name: Visual memory debugging
deliverables.description: Used vs free regions visible as different colors on texture

phases.title: Compilation Pipeline
phases.description:
Three-tier compilation: .gasm text assembly -> pixel bytecode,
GeoLang compiler (C-like -> pixel opcodes directly, 2079 lines, 25 tests),
and C-to-.glyph transpiler supporting arithmetic, control flow, structs, pointers, arrays.
phases.goal: Text assembly and C-like language to pixel bytecode compilation.

deliverables.name: Assembler (.gasm -> pixel bytecode)
deliverables.description: Two-pass assembler with labels, DATA directives, disassembler

acceptance_criteria.description: Labels resolve correctly, DATA sections load into texture

deliverables.name: GeoLang compiler
deliverables.description: C-like language compiled directly to pixel opcodes (2079 lines, 25 tests)

acceptance_criteria.description: Arithmetic, control flow, functions compile and execute

deliverables.name: C-to-.glyph transpiler
deliverables.description: Transpiles C: arithmetic, control flow, structs, pointers, arrays -> pixel bytecode

deliverables.name: GASP animation compiler
deliverables.description: Tween/animation system with keyframe labels (src/gasp_compiler.rs)

phases.title: IPC and I/O
phases.description:
SPAWN/YIELD for VM forking with memory isolation. SEND/RECV message queues
for inter-VM communication. 3-layer IPC: GPU VM -> device proxy .glyph shim -> CPU stub Rust thread.
CPU stub with 9 command types including file I/O, SQL queries, and LLM calls.
phases.goal: Inter-VM communication and the CPU stub pattern for external I/O.

deliverables.name: SPAWN/YIELD VM forking
deliverables.description: Create child VMs with isolated memory regions

acceptance_criteria.description: Child VM executes independently, memory isolated from parent

deliverables.name: SEND/RECV message queues
deliverables.description: Inter-VM message passing via mailbox system

deliverables.name: Event queue (WAIT_EVENT)
deliverables.description: External event injection for keyboard, mouse, inter-VM messages

deliverables.name: CPU stub with 9 command types
deliverables.description: READ_BLOCK, WRITE_BLOCK, OPEN, CLOSE, IOCTL, CMD_SQL_QUERY, CMD_MODEL_CALL, CMD_STATUS_READ, CMD_STATUS_WRITE

deliverables.name: SqliteExecutor
deliverables.description: rusqlite-backed SQL query execution via CPU stub

deliverables.name: ModelExecutor
deliverables.description: HTTP LLM client via CPU stub -- VM can call LLMs

deliverables.name: FileExecutor
deliverables.description: File I/O via CPU stub

phases.technical_notes:
The CPU stub pattern is what makes agent-driven execution possible:
the VM can call an LLM, query a database, or read a file without any
of those concepts existing in the GPU shader.

phases.title: Self-Improvement
phases.description:
The VM can evolve its own programs. Evolution engine (src/evolution.rs, 1045 lines)
performs mutation -> execute -> score -> keep/discard cycles. Fitness function
(src/fitness.rs) uses composite scoring: speed, correctness, memory, spatial locality.
Governance gate (src/governance.rs, 558 lines) enforces Seven Laws before any mutation.
phases.goal: Evolution engine with fitness-guided mutations and governance gates.

deliverables.name: Evolution engine (evolution.rs)
deliverables.description: Mutation -> execute -> score -> keep/discard loop

acceptance_criteria.description: GEO-68: fitness-component-guided mutations beat random by 57% on real GPU

deliverables.name: Fitness function (fitness.rs)
deliverables.description: Composite scoring: speed, correctness, memory, spatial locality

deliverables.name: Mutation engine (mutation.rs)
deliverables.description: Pixel-level variations with selection pressure

deliverables.name: Governance gate (governance.rs)
deliverables.description: Seven Laws check before any mutation executes (558 lines)

phases.title: Visualization
phases.description:
Real-time visualization of the 4096x4096 texture at 30fps. VM HUD overlay shows
register values, PC, execution state. Filmstrip projector captures multi-frame
execution for time-based debugging. 8x8 bitmap font atlas at 0x00F00000.
phases.goal: Windowed runtime with VM HUD overlay, filmstrip projector, and font atlas.

deliverables.name: Windowed runtime
deliverables.description: 4096x4096 texture display at 30fps via wgpu

deliverables.name: VM HUD overlay
deliverables.description: Register values, PC, execution state rendered spatially

deliverables.name: Filmstrip projector (filmstrip.rs)
deliverables.description: Multi-frame execution capture for time-based debugging

deliverables.name: Font atlas
deliverables.description: 8x8 bitmap font at 0x00F00000, CHAR opcode renders glyphs

phases.title: Infrastructure
phases.description:
DRM backend for AMDGPU with DMA-BUF sharing. Pixel compiler/linker with stdlib.
Multiple binaries: daemon, pmp-eval, pmp-repl, pmp-trace, frame_debug.
Canvas-based UI: 32x32 text surface, assembler, terminal mode (geo> CLI),
256x256 framebuffer. Old sprints A-E completed here.
phases.goal: DRM backend, pixel compiler/linker, multiple binary targets, and canvas-based UI.

deliverables.name: DRM backend
deliverables.description: AMDGPU command buffers, DMA-BUF sharing for zero-copy

deliverables.name: Pixel compiler/linker with stdlib
deliverables.description: Standard library: memset, memcpy, math routines loaded as pixel programs

deliverables.name: Multi-binary targets
deliverables.description: daemon, pmp-eval, pmp-repl, pmp-trace, frame_debug

deliverables.name: Canvas text surface + assembler
deliverables.description: 32x32 grid, type assembly, F8 to assemble, F5 to run

acceptance_criteria.description: All Sprint A-E visual and interactive programs work

deliverables.name: Terminal mode (geo> CLI)
deliverables.description: REPL with help, list/ls, load, run, edit, regs, peek, poke, reset, clear, quit

phases.title: Bare-Metal RV64
phases.description:
The current system runs through wgpu, which has limitations: no native u64 support
in WGSL (requiring vec2<u32> emulation), buffer size constraints, and an abstraction
layer between the shader and the hardware. Phase 8 goes direct to metal.

Port the VM to SPIR-V with native u64 support. AMDGPU direct command submission
via DRM ioctl. DMA-BUF framebuffer sharing for zero-copy visualization. RV64I base
instruction set with Sv39 MMU.
phases.goal: Boot a minimal RV64 Linux kernel on the GPU, bypassing wgpu entirely.

deliverables.name: SPIR-V RV64 kernel
deliverables.description: Port the VM to SPIR-V with native u64 registers

acceptance_criteria.description: SPIR-V shader compiles and dispatches on AMD hardware
Native u64 registers work (no vec2<u32> emulation)

deliverables.name: AMDGPU direct command submission
deliverables.description: PM4 packets, GVM management via DRM ioctl

acceptance_criteria.description: Submit compute dispatches directly to AMDGPU
acceptance_criteria.validation: DRM ioctl calls succeed, shader executes

deliverables.name: RV64I base instruction set
deliverables.description: Implement RV64I with Sv39 page table walks in the shader

acceptance_criteria.description: rv64ui-p-* compliance tests pass

deliverables.name: Boot minimal RV64 Linux
deliverables.description: The 'screen is the hard drive' concept fully realized

acceptance_criteria.description: Linux kernel boots on the GPU VM
acceptance_criteria.validation: Kernel boot messages visible on framebuffer

dependencies.reason: Need working wgpu-based system as reference implementation

phases.technical_notes:
Steps:
1. Create SPIR-V RV64 kernel (systems/infinite_map_rs/src/shaders/riscv64_vm.glsl)
2. Implement native u64 registers and Sv39 page table walks in the shader
3. Complete AMDGPU command buffer submission (PM4 packets, GVM management)
4. Boot minimal RV64 Linux on the native GPU executor
5. Verify with rv64ui-p-* compliance tests
risks: AMDGPU ioctl interface is poorly documented -- may need kernel module development
SPIR-V compute shaders have different capabilities than WGSL -- feature discovery needed
RV64I emulation performance on GPU compute units is unknown

phases.title: AI-Native Opcodes
phases.description:
The current opcode set is deterministic: ADD always adds, JMP always jumps.
But LLM-generated code is probabilistic. Phase 9 introduces opcodes for
uncertainty-aware execution, attention mechanisms, and self-modification.
phases.goal: Opcodes designed for probabilistic, AI-generated code execution.

deliverables.name: BRANCH_PROB (220)
deliverables.description: Branch based on probability threshold

acceptance_criteria.description: Branches correctly with given probability value

deliverables.name: CONFIDENCE_MARK (221)
deliverables.description: Associate confidence score with a code block

deliverables.name: ALTERNATE_PATH (222)
deliverables.description: Define fallback paths for low-confidence execution

deliverables.name: ATTENTION_FOCUS (223)
deliverables.description: Mark spatial regions as active, skip inactive regions

deliverables.name: GLYPH_MUTATE (224)
deliverables.description: Transform a glyph at a coordinate (self-modification)

deliverables.name: SPATIAL_SPAWN (225)
deliverables.description: Create new glyph clusters at target coordinates

deliverables.name: SEMANTIC_MERGE (226)
deliverables.description: Unify redundant glyph clusters (spatial refactoring)

deliverables.name: LOAD_EMBEDDING (227)
deliverables.description: Load vector embeddings into specialized registers

acceptance_criteria.description: Embedding loaded and accessible by other opcodes

dependencies.reason: Can be developed in parallel with Phase 8 but benefits from stable base
Self-improvement patterns inform AI-native opcode design

phases.technical_notes:
When an AI generates .glyph code, it doesn't know with certainty that every opcode
is correct. Probabilistic execution lets the VM handle uncertainty gracefully.
Attention focus lets it skip clearly wrong regions. Self-modification opcodes
let it fix its own output.
risks: Probabilistic opcodes make debugging harder -- need excellent visualization
Unclear how to test non-deterministic opcodes -- may need statistical testing

phases.title: Persistent Spatial Filesystem
phases.description:
Each file occupies a contiguous spatial region of the texture. Directory structure
is a spatial hierarchy (parent regions contain child regions). Metadata stored as
pixel headers. CPU stub's existing file I/O handles persistence to disk. Hilbert
curve ensures spatial locality for related files.
phases.goal: Files as spatial regions of the texture, not traditional block devices.

deliverables.name: Spatial file allocator
deliverables.description: Allocate/free spatial regions for file storage in the texture

acceptance_criteria.description: Files can be created, read, written, and deleted
Spatial locality maintained for related files

deliverables.name: Directory hierarchy
deliverables.description: Parent regions contain child regions with metadata headers

acceptance_criteria.description: Nested directory traversal works

deliverables.name: Persistence bridge
deliverables.description: CPU stub syncs spatial regions to/from disk

acceptance_criteria.description: Files survive VM restart

deliverables.name: Visual file browser
deliverables.description: See your files on the texture -- opening a file is navigating to a spatial region

dependencies.reason: Need stable memory allocation and CPU stub for persistence
Hilbert curve mapping is the foundation of spatial file addressing

risks: Fragmentation of spatial regions over time -- need spatial defragmentation
File size limited by available texture space (64MB total)

phases.title: Networking as Spatial I/O
phases.description:
New CPU stub commands: CMD_NET_SEND (10), CMD_NET_RECV (11), CMD_NET_DNS (12).
Network addresses represented as spatial coordinates. Agent loops can query
external services, fetch data, communicate with other instances. The VM doesn't
know about TCP/IP -- it writes a message and reads the response.
phases.goal: Network communication through the CPU stub pattern.

deliverables.name: Network CPU stub commands
deliverables.description: CMD_NET_SEND, CMD_NET_RECV, CMD_NET_DNS in the stub executor

acceptance_criteria.description: VM can send/receive messages to/from network endpoints

deliverables.name: Spatial address mapping
deliverables.description: Network endpoints mapped to spatial coordinates

deliverables.name: DNS resolution
deliverables.description: VM can resolve hostnames via CPU stub

dependencies.reason: CPU stub pattern is the I/O mechanism

phases.title: Self-Hosting
phases.description:
The ultimate proof of the architecture. If the compiler can run on the VM it
targets, the system has achieved self-hosting. Same milestone GCC and Rust
achieved for traditional platforms.
phases.goal: The GeoLang compiler, compiled to .glyph, running on the GPU VM.

deliverables.name: Compiler self-compilation
deliverables.description: Verify GeoLang compiler can compile itself (self-compilation)

acceptance_criteria.description: Compiler output matches reference build

deliverables.name: Compiler transpiled to .glyph
deliverables.description: Transpile the compiler's own C output to .glyph bytecode

deliverables.name: Compiler loaded into GPU texture
deliverables.description: The compiled compiler runs on the GPU VM

acceptance_criteria.description: VM can assemble programs using the on-GPU compiler

dependencies.reason: Need working GeoLang compiler and transpiler
Filesystem helpful for managing compiler output on the texture

phases.technical_notes:
Steps:
1. Verify the GeoLang compiler can compile itself (self-compilation)
2. Transpile the compiler's own C output to .glyph bytecode
3. Load the compiled compiler into the GPU texture
4. The VM assembles its own programs -- the system builds itself
risks: Compiler may be too large for a single VM's memory region
Performance of compiler on GPU VM may be impractically slow

phases.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
phases.description:
Specialized agents on separate VMs: researcher (queries LLM for ideas),
coder (generates .glyph), tester (runs programs and scores fitness), reviewer (governance gate).
GPU-parallel evolution: population of mutants evaluated simultaneously across 8 VMs.
phases.goal: Multiple agent loops running concurrently on different VMs, coordinated via IPC.

deliverables.name: Agent role definitions
deliverables.description: Researcher, Coder, Tester, Reviewer -- each on its own VM

acceptance_criteria.description: Each agent role executes its specialized task independently

deliverables.name: Inter-agent IPC protocol
deliverables.description: SEND/RECV message queue protocol for agent coordination

acceptance_criteria.description: Agents can delegate tasks and share results via IPC

deliverables.name: GPU-parallel evolution
deliverables.description: Population of mutants evaluated simultaneously across 8 VMs

acceptance_criteria.description: 8 VMs evaluate different mutants in parallel

deliverables.name: Agent-driven self-improvement loop
deliverables.description: The multi-agent system runs autonomously, improving the codebase

acceptance_criteria.description: System produces measurably improved programs without human intervention

dependencies.reason: IPC and CPU stub are the communication backbone
Evolution engine and fitness functions are the core mechanism
AI-native oppcodes would improve agent-generated code quality
Self-hosting means agents can modify the compiler itself

risks: Agent coordination complexity -- deadlock and starvation possible
Resource contention across 8 VMs for GPU time
Governance enforcement across multiple agents is non-trivial

global_risks: wgpu API changes between versions may break shader compatibility
GPU compute shader precision varies across vendors -- NVIDIA-only for now
64MB texture limit constrains program complexity and filesystem size
Self-hosting bootstrap problem: compiler needs itself to build itself
Regulatory/ethical concerns around autonomous self-improving GPU programs
conventions: All opcodes tested on both software_vm.rs and GPU shader
Rust code formatted with rustfmt, linted with clippy
347 tests must remain green -- no regressions allowed
New opcodes require: implementation, tests, documentation, assembler support
CPU stub executors are independently testable
Spatial locality principle: related data goes in adjacent texture regions
The 'screen is the hard drive' -- framebuffer regions map to persistent data
